beats.title: Kore cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Learning that a single out-of-memory message hides two completely different failures finally told me why my fixes kept missing. Using weight memory for flash and largest-single-activation for SRAM: (1) walk me through why SRAM is driven by the biggest live activation rather than the sum of all of them, (2) show me how the INT8 toggle flips flash from fail to pass while SRAM barely moves, (3) tell me what mitigation, tiling or restructuring, I'd reach for when it's SRAM, not flash, that blows the ceiling.

props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · CLAUDE CODE · MEMORY VERDICT
props.segment: Build a Memory Verdict Module with

beats.narration_text: Humanitarians AI. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

props.command: Build a Memory Verdict Module with Claude Code
props.segment: Build a Memory Verdict Module with Claude Code

beats.role_note: the problem, the context, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Flash holds the weights; SRAM holds the activations. When a model doesn't fit, the error message says out of memory -- and that single message hides which one failed. A flash problem and an SRAM problem have different fixes: compress weights for flash, tile or restructure activations for SRAM. The verdict module names which one broke.
beats.visual_intent: title card: FLASH (weights) vs SRAM (activations); out of memory as two different bugs with two different fixes

beats.narration_text: Ask for the experiment. In the terminal: claude -- write memory dot py, inputs param count, dtype, firmware kilobytes, largest activation, target flash and SRAM kilobytes, emit a typed verdict with flash margin, SRAM margin, and mitigations.

props.command:
claude "write memory.py: inputs param_count, dtype,
  firmware_kb, largest_activation, target
  flash_kb + sram_kb. Emit Verdict(fits,
  flash_margin, sram_margin, mitigations[])."
props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · CLAUDE CODE · MEMORY VERDICT

beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. Weight memory is params times bytes per param -- int8 is one byte, FP32 is four. SRAM is driven by the largest single activation, not the sum -- because only one layer is live at a time. The verdict separates the two margins and names a different mitigation for each.

props.code:
# memory.py  --  Claude Code output
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List

@dataclass
class Verdict:
    fits: bool
    flash_margin_kb: float
    sram_margin_kb: float
    mitigations: List[str] = field(default_factory=list)

def check_memory(param_count, dtype, firmware_kb,
                 largest_act_kb, flash_kb, sram_kb):
    bpp = {"fp32": 4, "int8": 1, "int16": 2}.get(dtype, 4)
    weight_kb = param_count * bpp / 1024
    flash_used = weight_kb + firmware_kb
    flash_margin = flash_kb - flash_used
    sram_margin  = sram_kb - largest_act_kb
    mits = []
    if flash_margin < 0: mits.append("quantize weights")
    if sram_margin  < 0: mits.append("tile activations")
    return Verdict(flash_margin >= 0 and sram_margin >= 0,
                   flash_margin, sram_margin, mits)
props.title: memory.py

beats.narration_text: Run it. A MobileNetV2-0.5 in FP32: the flash bar fills to 5.6 megabytes against a 4-megabyte ceiling -- over budget, crimson. The SRAM bar at 0.5 megabytes sits safely below its 1-megabyte ceiling -- ink. Two bars, two different stories. The verdict says flash fails, SRAM is fine.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: FLASH bar at x=-2.5 (5.6MB, CRIMSON, over 4MB ceiling), SRAM bar at x=2.5 (0.5MB, INK, under 1MB ceiling), ceiling lines SLATE, chip FLASH:FAIL SRAM:OK

beats.narration_text: That's the FP32 picture. The fix is int8 quantization. Iterate: claude -- add int8 as a toggle and re-emit the verdict -- watch flash pass, SRAM barely move.

props.command:
claude "add int8 as a toggle and re-emit the
  verdict -- watch flash pass, SRAM barely move"
props.topic: HUMANITARIANS AI · CLAUDE CODE · MEMORY VERDICT

beats.narration_text: Re-run. INT8 toggle: flash drops to 1.4 megabytes, now under the 4-megabyte ceiling -- ink. SRAM barely moves, down to 0.25 megabytes -- still fine. The verdict flips from flash fail to flash pass. SRAM was never the problem; int8 fixed the one that was.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: same layout, FLASH bar now 1.4MB (INK, under ceiling), SRAM bar 0.25MB (INK), chip FLASH:PASS SRAM:PASS

beats.narration_text: Out of memory is two different bugs. Flash fails when weights plus firmware exceeds the budget -- compress weights. SRAM fails when the largest activation exceeds the budget -- tile or restructure. A typed verdict forces you to name which. Naming which tells you the fix.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: FLASH FAIL -> compress weights; SRAM FAIL -> tile activations; typed verdict as the diagnostic

beats.narration_text: Your move: pull your model's param count and largest-activation size from the toolkit loader, and your target's flash and SRAM from the datasheet. Run the verdict. If flash fails, int8 is the first lever. If SRAM fails, layer-by-layer tiling is the next question.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) get param count + largest activation from model loader, 2) get flash/SRAM from datasheet, 3) run the verdict

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Out of memory is two different bugs. Flash fails when weights plus firmware exceeds the budget -- compress weights. SRAM fails when the largest activation exceeds the budget -- tile or restructure. A typed verdict forces you to name which. Naming which tells you the fix. Your move: pull your model's param count and largest-activation size from the toolkit loader, and your target's flash and SRAM from the datasheet. Run the verdict. If flash fails, int8 is the first lever. If SRAM fails, layer-by-layer tiling is the next question.
